**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12th, 1894*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, the sea a churning, foaming thing that seems to want to swallow the very cliffs we’ve built our homes upon. The storm is coming, and I fear for my life, but more than that, I fear for the silence that has grown between us.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I do not know if you remember me at all, or if you have forgotten the man who once held you in his arms when you were a child, who taught you the stars, who watched over you with a father’s pride. I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. Not in the least.

Every night, I sit by the light, the great beacon that guides the ships safely home, and I think of you. I wonder if you ever thought of me, if you ever looked up at the stars and remembered the stories I told you. I wonder if you still believe in the power of light, even in the darkest of nights.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The sea roars like a thousand voices, and the wind sings a mournful tune through the cracks in the stone. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the kind of letter you were hoping to receive. I have not written to you in five long years, and I do not know if I will have the chance to write again. But tonight, as the storm gathers on the horizon, I feel the weight of time pressing upon me like the sea against the cliffs.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even when you were not here, even when we did not speak, you were the one who called me home. I remember the day you left—how the wind howled like a wolf at the door, and your eyes held a fire that I could not understand. You said you needed to find yourself, and I let you go. I thought it was for the best. I thought I would see you again. But the years have passed like the tide, and I have not seen your face in the mirror since.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have known. The sky is black, the sea is restless, and the wind sings a song of sorrow. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but I wonder if it will be enough. I have always believed that the light would guide the lost, but I fear that tonight, I may be the one who is lost.

I have no regrets, Eleanor. I have lived my life with purpose, with honor, and

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea roars, and the sky is black as a tomb, but I must speak before the night swallows me whole.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have not spoken to you, nor have you spoken to me. I have kept the light burning, though my heart has gone out like the lantern’s wick in the storm. You were once my light, my only light. Now, I am but a shadow, and the sea is my only companion.

I know you think me cruel, that I have let the years pass without a word. But I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the day you left, the way your eyes filled with tears and your voice cracked as you said, *"I can't stay here anymore, Papa."* I tried to hold you close, but you pulled away, and the door closed behind you, and the silence has never left.

Tonight, the storm is worse than any I've seen. The waves are like the claws of some great beast, and the wind screams as if it knows the truth. I have no doubt that I may not see dawn. And yet, I must write this, for I have something to say.

If I do not survive, know this: I have not forgotten you

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not presume. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I find myself penning these words with a heavy heart and a mind clouded by the relentless winds that howl through the lighthouse. The sea has been restless of late, and the storm that is set to descend upon us tonight is said to be the worst in decades. I have little doubt that I shall not see dawn.

I write to you now, not out of hope for your reply, but out of a need to leave behind something of myself should the worst come to pass. You see, I have always been a man of duty, and though I have not been the best father, I have been faithful to my post. I hope that this letter will find you, and that it will bring with it a piece of me, even if I do not.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I ever will again. The world has changed in your absence, I dares not say how. You are no longer the little girl who used to sit by the window and watch the waves, but a woman of your own making. I pray that you have found your way in life, and that you have not been as lonely as I have been.

I have not been able to visit you, and I have not been able to write, but I have not forgotten you

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**It has been five long years since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and I write these words now with a heart heavy with regret, sorrow, and the weight of time. You are not the only one who has changed, my child, but I fear I have changed the most.**

**I sit here, in this lonely tower, with the sea howling like a beast in the night, and the wind tearing at the very stones of this old place as if the very earth itself resists the coming storm. The storm is coming, and I fear it may not spare me. I have seen the sky turn black with fury, and the sea rise like a beast awakened from a long slumber. I have no doubt that if the Lord wills it, I shall not see another dawn. And so, I write this letter, not with the hope that it may reach you, but with the certainty that it must.**

**You were a child when I last saw you, and I have not seen you since that day you left this house, with your mother, with your tears and your anger. I did not mean to wound you, Eliza, and I have not spoken of it since. I thought, in my foolishness, that I could protect you from the world, that I could shield you from the cruel truths of life. But I was wrong. I was

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside these stone walls, and the sea roars as though it, too, mourns the silence between us. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear the storm that now approaches may be the last thing I speak of.

You were once my light, Eleanor—my guiding star through the darkest nights. I remember the days when you would come to visit, your laughter echoing through these halls like the sound of the waves on a calm sea. We spoke of everything and nothing, and in those moments, I felt the warmth of a father's love, even if it was not always the way you wished to see it. But time, my dear, is a cruel thing. It does not wait for the heart to mend, nor does it forgive the wounds we carry.

I have not written because I feared what might be said, and I have not spoken because I feared what might be heard. You were always so sharp-tongued, so quick to take offense, and I, in my pride, allowed myself to believe that your silence was a sign that I had done something wrong. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was very, very wrong.

The storm is coming, and I do not know if I will see the dawn. The lighthouse has stood for generations, a

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay it finds me in a state of desolation, for I have not heard from you in five long years, and the silence has grown heavier than the sea's wrath. It is with a heavy heart and a trembling hand that I write these words, for I fear the night ahead may be the last I shall see.

The storm is upon us, and it is a most terrible one. The wind howls like the wails of the damned, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, has turned upon me with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse stands firm, as it always has, but I am but a man, frail and mortal, and I fear the gods may be displeased with me.

I have watched the sky darken with a foreboding that seems to echo the weight of my own soul. The waves rise like mountains, crashing against the rocks with a might that shakes the very earth. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for thirty years now, and the light shines forth, a beacon of hope for those who sail these treacherous waters. Yet, as I stand upon the narrow platform, I cannot help but feel that I am but a candle in the wind, and that the darkness may yet consume me.

I have often thought of you, my dear Eleanor, and the days when you were but a child, running

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The storm is upon us, a beast of wind and fury that howls like the ghost of my own sorrow. I have not spoken your name in five years, and yet, as the sea roars and the lighthouse stands defiant against the night, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in all that time.

You were a child of light, Eleanor. Bright, curious, and full of questions that could not be answered by any man, not even your father. You asked me why the lighthouse was built, why I had to stay here, why I could not be with you. And I gave you answers that were not true. I told you I had to be here to protect the coast, to guard the ships, to keep the sea in check. But the truth is, I stayed here because I could not face the world beyond this rock, because I was afraid of what I might find, of what I might lose.

You left me, Eleanor. Not with words, but with silence. You did not write, you did not call, you did not come. And I did not go to you. I told myself that it was for your own good, that the world was too cruel, too cold, too full of things that would not understand you. But I was wrong. I was wrong to let you go, and I was wrong to let myself

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like the wails of the damned outside. The sea is rising, the sky is black as a widow’s veil, and the light from the beacon has flickered for the last time. I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must say these things before I am claimed by the waves.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left, and five years since I stopped writing. I have not spoken your name aloud, not even in the silence of the lighthouse. I have not spoken to you at all. You were not the first to leave, and you will not be the last. But you were mine, and I would have given the world to keep you.

I know you think me a monster. That I abandoned you. But I did not. I stayed. I stood in the cold, the rain, the dark, for you. I kept the light burning when the world turned its back. I kept it burning so you would not be lost. I kept it burning so you would not be alone.

You were a child, Eliza. A child who needed more than I could give. I was a man who had no answers, no words, no love to spare. I tried to be a father, but I failed. I tried to be a husband, but I was not. I tried to be a man, and I was not.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of a reply, but with the weight of a heart that has long since grown heavy with unspoken words. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the years have worn me down, they have not dulled the ache of your absence. I have not written to you, nor have you written to me, and I fear that time has begun to erode what little remains of our bond.

The sea has been restless these past weeks, and tonight, the storm is coming. The winds howl like the wails of a soul in torment, and the waves, fierce and unrelenting, crash against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost human. I have spent the evening pacing the tower, the lantern’s light flickering like a dying ember, and I find myself thinking of you more than I ever have in all these years.

You were always the storm in the family—wild, unyielding, and full of a fire that no one could quite understand. I remember the nights you would climb to the top of the tower with me, your laughter echoing through the stone walls, and the way the sea would calm in your presence. You had a way of making the world feel smaller, and the lighthouse feel like a sanctuary. But now, the world feels vast and empty, and the lighthouse feels like a tomb.

I have not been able to let go of you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with both sorrow and hope. The ink is barely dry upon the page, and yet the words seem to falter before they can reach you. Five years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and though the years have worn upon me, it is the absence of your presence that has aged me most.

You were but a child when you left, and now you are a woman of your own making, I daresay. I do not know what path you have taken, nor do I care to guess, for I have long since ceased to think of you as the little girl who once ran through the dunes with her father's coat flapping behind her. That girl is gone, and in her place lingers only the echo of a mother’s love, a father’s regret, and a son’s silence.

But tonight... tonight is different. The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind has grown sharp as a blade. The waves have risen like the wrath of some vengeful god, and the sky is painted in shades of black and crimson, as though the heavens themselves weep for what has been lost. I have seen storms before, Eleanor, but none like this. The lighthouse has stood for over a century, and I have watched countless tempests pass, but this... this is a storm that whispers of endings.

I have not spoken

My Dearest Eleanor,

It has been five years since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and yet the weight of that silence still sits heavy upon my shoulders, like the salt air that clings to my skin after a storm. I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls outside my window like a banshee, and I fear that this night may be the last.

You were a child when you left me, and I was a fool to let you go. I have spent these years in this lighthouse, watching the sea and the sky, trying to find meaning in the endless horizon. But the sea never speaks, and the sky never answers. I have been alone, but I have not been empty. Until now.

This storm is unlike any I've seen. The waves are not merely crashing against the rocks—they are *roaring*. I have seen the light flicker, and I fear that the lamp may not survive the night. I have lived my life in service to this beacon, and I have always believed that it was worth it. But now, I wonder if it was all just a way to avoid facing the truth: that I have never truly been able to keep you safe, and that I have never been the father you deserved.

I have always believed that you would return, that you would come back when you were ready. But I have not waited. I have let time pass, and I have let my heart harden. I have

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12, 1893*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the glass of my window. The sea has risen in fury, and the lighthouse, though steadfast, groans under the weight of the storm. I have not spoken to you in five years, not since the day you left, and yet now, as the night falls and the tempest gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, not to you, but to the memory of you.

You were a child of the sea, Eleanor, as I was. You would run through the dunes at dawn, your laughter like the sound of waves on the shore. I remember how you used to climb the tower, peering out at the horizon, and I would tell you stories of the sailors who had perished in the dark, of the ships that had vanished without a trace. You would ask me, "Did they find peace, Papa?" and I would say, "Only if they saw the light."

But the light, my dear, is not enough. It guides, it warns, it shines, but it cannot comfort the soul. And so it is with me, Eleanor. I have been a lighthouse keeper for twenty years, but I have not been a father for fifteen.

I know you have been hurt, and I know I

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 13th, 1892*  
*St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly*  

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand, for the wind howls like the ghost of a thousand lost souls, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth beneath us. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse. I have not spoken your name in five years, and yet it lingers in my thoughts like the scent of the sea at dusk.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and of silence where words might not have done. I have tended this light for twenty years, through every season, every storm, every night that the world forgot its way. And yet, I have not tended your heart. I have not reached out, not even once. I have let the years pass like the tides, and I have not turned back.

I know the years have been hard on you. I know the world has moved on, and that I have not been the father you needed. I have been a man of stone, when you needed a man of warmth. I have been a keeper of the light, when you needed a father who would have walked with you through the dark.

But now, as the storm gathers, I feel the weight of my silence.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient stone walls. The sea roars as if it too has heard of your name, and I fear the storm that gathers on the horizon will be more than a mere tempest—it may be the end of me.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not written to you, nor have you written to me. I have not spoken of the things that have kept me from you—my pride, my silence, the weight of the lighthouse on my shoulders. But tonight, as the heavens darken and the sea grows restless, I find myself unable to hold back the truth any longer.

You were but a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too large for the world. I tried to reach you, to remind you of the stories we once shared by the fire, the nights when you would sit with me and listen to the sea's lullaby. But you were not content to stay. You wanted more than the quiet life of a lighthouse keeper's daughter. You wanted the world.

And I, in my arrogance, thought I could keep you here. That I could keep you safe. But I was wrong. You were always meant to go.

I have spent these years in silence, watching the light burn and the waves crash, wondering if you would ever come back. I

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you at all. The winds are howling like the devil himself, and the sea is churning with a fury I've never seen before. I write this with trembling hands, for I know I may not live to see the dawn. But I must try, for I have much to say and little time.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not spoken to you since. I have not spoken to anyone. I have been a solitary soul, tending to this lighthouse and the sea, which has been my only companion. I have kept the fire burning day and night, for I know that the sailors must have light, even if it is not for me.

I have thought often of you, Eleanor. I have missed your laughter, your voice, your presence. I have missed the way you used to help me with the chores, and the way you would sit by the window, reading to me. I have missed you more than I can say. I have even missed your terrible temper, for it was better than the silence.

But I know you have not missed me. I know you have moved on, perhaps with a husband, perhaps with a new life. I have heard from the sailors that you have taken up a position in the city, perhaps in the theater, or maybe in the parlors of the rich. I have heard

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1887**

**Dear Eleanor,**

I write to you now, in the dim light of this lighthouse, where the wind howls like the souls of the damned and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth beneath me. The storm is upon us, and I fear it will not spare me. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I would not leave this world without one last word to you.

You have not answered my letters, nor have you sent word of your own. I suppose I should not be surprised. You have always been a woman of your own mind, and I, a man of my own stubbornness. I have not written to you because I feared what might come of it—what might come *from* it. And I suppose, in my way, I have been right.

But now, as the sky darkens and the wind begins to howl like a wounded beast, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. You were once my greatest pride, my most cherished joy. You were the light in my life, the fire that kept me from drowning in the cold, unyielding sea of duty and loneliness. And now, as the storm gathers, I find myself wondering if I have been too harsh, too distant, too much of a fool to see what you were

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that even I, with my years of experience, find unnerving. I write this with trembling hands, for the storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent them in silence, save for the crackling of the fire and the echo of the waves. I have not written, nor have I called, and I know not if that is because I have been afraid, or if it is because you have chosen to forget me. I have been a fool, Eleanor, and I have been a coward. I have let pride and pride alone stand between us, and for that, I am sorry. I do not know if you will forgive me, but I must try.

You were always the one who questioned the world, who saw more than the surface, who looked beyond the horizon. I, on the other hand, was content to stand upon the shore and watch the tide. I was not a man of words, nor a man of great deeds, and I have made a terrible mistake in thinking that was enough. I have been a poor father, a poor husband, and a poor man. I have let you go, and I have let myself go too

My Dearest Eleanor,

It has been five long years since I last set pen to paper, and longer still since I last saw your face. Time, like the sea, has a way of eroding even the sturdiest of hearts, and I fear I have let it do so. Yet, as the wind howls through the cliffs and the waves rise like vengeful spirits, I find myself compelled to write, not only to the sea, but to you.

You were once the light in my life, the gentle flame that guided me through the darkest nights of both storm and soul. But that flame has flickered, and I have been too proud, too stubborn, to let it dim. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know that I have wronged you. I know that my silence has been a wound, and I pray that you may forgive me, though I do not know if I deserve it.

Tonight, the sky is heavy with clouds, and the air is thick with the scent of impending rain. The storm is coming, and I am not sure I will see the dawn. The lighthouse stands tall, but even it may falter before the fury of the elements. I have spent my life watching the sea, and I have learned that it is not the storm that is the enemy, but the fear of it. And so, I must face it, not with despair, but with the resolve that has kept me here

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink that has long dried on my fingertips, the quill I have not lifted in five years, and the heart that has grown heavier with each passing day. The sea has always been my companion, but tonight, it is my enemy. The storm brews on the horizon, a dark mass of fury that whispers of the end. I do not know if I will see the dawn.

You were twelve when you left me, and I did not stop you. I told you to go, to find your own path, to live your life. I believed it was for the best. I believed you would return. But you did not. You have not written, you have not called, and you have not come. I have been alone in this tower, save for the wind and the waves, and the ghosts of the ones I have watched drown in the dark.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will have the strength to say anything at all. But I must write this, for it is the only way I can make sense of the silence between us.

You were my light. When the world was too bright, you were my shadow. When the world was too dark, you were my flame. You were the daughter I had not expected, but who came to me with the scent of salt and the sound of laughter, as if the sea had chosen you as its

**To My Dearest Elara,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The wind howls like a banshee outside, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never again. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, as if the very air demands it.

You were once my greatest joy, my little light in the darkness. I remember the days when you would climb the stairs to the lantern room, your small fingers clutching the railing, your eyes wide with wonder as you watched the waves crash against the rocks below. You would ask me, "Why do the lights shine so bright, Papa?" And I would tell you, "Because they guide the lost, just as you guide me, Elara."

But time, like the tide, has worn away the edges of our bond. I know I have been a poor father, a man too stubborn to speak the words that would have mended what was broken. I let pride and silence build a wall between us, and I have paid the price. You have grown into a woman of strength and grace, while I have become a relic of the past, a man who cannot even offer you a proper goodbye.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can say what I must. I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles—not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken and the burden of a heart that has longed for your return. The storm that brews beyond the cliffs is but a whisper to the tempest within me, and I find myself at the edge of both the sea and my soul.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent, and though time has weathered the edges of our once-close bond, I have never ceased to think of you. You were but a child when you left, and though I have seen many seasons pass, I have not seen a single day without your name on my lips or your face in my dreams.

The lighthouse stands firm, though the wind howls like a wounded beast. The beacon burns bright, as it always has, a steadfast guide for those who sail these waters. Yet tonight, the sky is heavy with storm, and the waves grow wilder by the moment. I have watched the sea for hours, and in its fury, I have seen the reflection of my own heart—fractured, unmoored, and yearning for something I cannot name.

I have always believed that the sea is a mirror, and in its depths, one might find the truth of oneself. But I fear that in my case, the mirror has shattered, and I can no longer see the reflection of who I once was—or who I hope to be

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart both heavy and full, for the storm that looms upon the horizon is not the only tempest that has gathered in my soul. Five years have passed since our last exchange, and I find myself wondering if the winds of time have carried you far from this place—or if you remain, as I once did, bound to the past.

You were but a child when we last parted, and though I have tended this lighthouse with unwavering devotion, I have often found myself longing for the days when your laughter would echo through these stone halls. I have heard the sea’s roar from the cliffs, and I have watched the stars through the narrow window, but never have I felt so alone as I do now. The lamps burn bright, and the beacon stands firm, yet my spirit feels as though it might falter in the face of this coming storm.

I have not written to you in so long, and I fear that if I do not speak now, I may never find the words to do so again. You must know that I have never ceased to think of you. I have not forgotten the days when we would sit by the hearth, and you would ask me questions about the sea, and I would tell you stories of the whales and the mermaids, and the ghost ships that sail the night. I have told those tales many times, but they are not the same without your eyes upon

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, for the storm that now rages beyond these walls has stirred something in me that I thought long buried. It is not the wind that unsettles me, but the silence that has lingered between us for five long years—silence that once felt like a chasm, and now, in the face of the unknown, feels like a companion.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will speak again. But tonight, as the sea howls and the sky weeps, I feel the need to say something. Perhaps it is only the storm that stirs such thoughts, but I fear it is more than that.

You were once my greatest joy, my greatest pride. I remember the day you left, and I remember the way your mother looked at me, as if I had done something unforgivable. I do not know if I have ever truly explained myself to you, or if I ever would have. I have spent these years in the company of the sea, and in the quiet of this lighthouse, trying to make sense of what I did—or did not do.

I have always believed that love is not something that can be measured in words, but I have come to think that perhaps, in the absence of words, love can be measured in silence. And I have carried that silence like a stone on my chest, heavier with each passing year.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*To My Dearest Eleanor,*

I sit here by the window of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, the sea roiling in fury, and my hands trembling as I write. The lantern above me flickers with the storm’s breath, and I wonder if it will hold. I wonder if I will.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of solitude, of watching the waves crash against the rocks, and of wondering if you ever thought of me. I have not spoken to you, not written, not even sent a card. I have let the years pass, like the tides, without a word.

But tonight is different, Eleanor. Tonight, the storm is coming with a vengeance, and I fear for my life. I have no one to turn to but the sea, and the sea has never been kind to me. I have no one to call for help, no one to pray with me, no one to say, "I love you," when the light goes out.

You were the light in my life, Eleanor. You were the warmth in the cold, the song in the silence, the reason I kept the lantern burning when the world seemed to have forgotten it. And now, I wonder if I have been a fool to let it go out.

I have no regrets, only grief. I have no words to undo the past, only the hope that perhaps

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better spirits than I have been these past five years. I have written many letters, but none have ever felt as heavy as this one. You see, I have not spoken to you in such a time, and I fear that the silence has grown more profound than the sea that surrounds this lighthouse.

The wind has been rising all day, and the sea has grown restless. The waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have not seen in years. I have been watching the horizon for the last few hours, and I cannot shake the feeling that something is coming—something terrible. The sky has turned a dark, stormy grey, and the air is thick with the scent of rain and salt. **To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from fear, but from the weight of years and the burden of silence. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and I have not spoken to you in all that time. The sea has been kinder than I have been to you, and yet, I find myself writing to you now, as the storm gathers on the horizon and the wind howls like a wounded beast.

You were but a girl when you left, and now you are a woman I scarcely recognize. The world has moved on, and so have you. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to reach out, to call, to write, but the words would not come. Perhaps they never would. Perhaps I was too afraid to speak the truth.

You see, I have not been the same man since the night you left. The sea is a cruel mistress, and it took more than it gave. I have watched the light burn through the years, and with it, I have watched my heart grow cold. I have not been a good father, nor a good man. I have let the world pass me by, and I have let you go.

But tonight is different, Eleanor. Tonight, the storm is coming. The sky is black as pitch, and the waves are rising like the wrath of God himself. I have no illusions—I may not make it through this night.
